did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a modified Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with GLCM and Energy (GLCME) features for skin cancer detection. The method involves pre-processing dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset, extracting handcrafted texture features, and feeding them into a deep learning model with ReLU and SoftMax activation functions to classify benign and malignant skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a modified Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with GLCME features for skin cancer detection. It details a specific methodology including data collection from the ISIC dataset (33,126 training and 10,982 testing images), pre-processing steps (adaptive median filter, bottom hat filtering), feature extraction, and model architecture. It reports specific results, including an accuracy of 98% achieved in 45 epochs, and compares these findings with prior works in Table 2. Although published in a conference proceedings (ODICON 2024), it contains original research results rather than being a review or survey.

title: Skin Cancer Detection using modified CNN with GLCME Features
journal: 2024 3rd Odisha International Conference on Electrical Power Engineering, Communication and Computing Technology (ODICON)
brief_summary: This study proposes a modified Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix and Energy (GLCME) features for automated skin cancer detection. The researchers utilized the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, comprising 33,126 training and 10,982 testing dermoscopic images. Pre-processing steps included adaptive median filtering and bottom hat filtering to remove artifacts like hair while preserving lesion features. The model architecture integrated handcrafted GLCME features with CNN outputs using concatenation layers, ReLU, and SoftMax activation functions. The proposed method achieved a diagnostic accuracy of 98% over 45 epochs, outperforming several comparative models listed in the results. The findings suggest that combining learned and handcrafted texture features enhances the accuracy of distinguishing benign from malignant skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant type in the ISIC dataset; 584 malignant images used in training.
Mentioned in introduction as types of skin cancer; BCC and SCC listed as specific types in the introduction section.
Classified as benign type in the ISIC dataset; 32,542 benign images used in training.

all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification to distinguish between benign and malignant skin cancer types using a modified CNN with GLCME features. The final layer uses a SoftMax activation function for normalized output, and the loss function is sparse categorical cross-entropy. The task involves classifying dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant).

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A public database of dermoscopic images used for skin cancer detection. The study utilized images in JPG format from this dataset.

use.training: Used for training the modified CNN model with GLCME features. The training set consisted of 33,126 images (32,542 benign and 584 malignant).
use.testing: Used for testing the model. The testing set consisted of 10,982 images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports accuracy metrics (98%) and compares them descriptively with prior works in a table.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.experiment: Proposed Modified CNN with GLCME Features
ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: The paper reports an accuracy of 98% achieved in 45 epochs. It compares this result against six prior works listed in Table 2. The paper mentions Sensitivity and Specificity as metrics but does not provide specific numerical values for them in the text or tables, only stating that Table 1 provides these metrics (though Table 1 content is not explicitly detailed with numbers in the provided context, only the header is visible). The model outperformed the listed baselines.

architecture_details.feature_integration: Hybrid approach combining CNN learned features with handcrafted Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and Energy (GLCME) features
layers: Flattening Layer (converts 2D feature maps to 1D vector)
Concatenation Layer (integrates CNN output with GLCM features)
activation_functions: SoftMax (used in the final fully connected layer for normalized output)
architecture_details.normalization: Batch normalization used to avoid overfitting

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully Connected Layer with SoftMax activation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Modified Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with GLCME features
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of color images to grayscale
Adaptive median filtering (3x3 filter size) for noise reduction and hair artifact removal
Bottom hat filtering for removal of unwanted hairs
Morphological operations for removing small objects and enhancing the image
Standardization of input images to a fixed size of 128x128 pixels
Scaling of pixel values to a common range between 0 and 1
conclusions: The proposed hybrid method combining a modified Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix and Energy (GLCME) features achieves a diagnostic accuracy of 98% on the ISIC dataset.

The model outperforms several comparative baseline methods, including DCNN+WCA (97.03%), DCNN+ Back Propagation (96.34%), FCM + CNN (92%), 3 Layered CNN (95.98%), CNN Model (97.08%), and CNN method (97.65%).

The adaptive median filter (3x3) effectively reduces hair artifacts in dermoscopic images while preserving critical skin features such as texture, pigmentation, and lesion edges, with minimal distortion.
Combining learned CNN features with handcrafted GLCME texture features enhances the ability to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions by capturing subtle textural differences.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the pre-processing step, specifically potential challenges with extremely fine hair or complex backgrounds, though these are considered manageable.

Future work includes improving pre-processing by combining adaptive median filtering with other techniques, verifying the technique on different medical images, and extending the approach to patient management and further diagnostic tests.
important_remarks: The dataset is severely imbalanced, with 32,542 benign images versus only 584 malignant images in the training set (ratio ~55:1), which may bias the model towards the benign class despite the high overall accuracy.

The paper does not report specific numerical values for Sensitivity or Specificity, only stating that Table 1 provides them; this lack of detailed performance metrics for the minority class (malignant) is a significant omission for clinical relevance.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the statistical significance of the 98% accuracy compared to baselines.
The study relies on a single public dataset (ISIC) without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting the generalizability of the findings to diverse clinical populations.

The pre-processing pipeline (adaptive median filter, bottom hat filtering, Otsu segmentation) may introduce data leakage or bias if parameters were tuned on the test set, though the paper does not explicitly detail the split strategy for hyperparameter tuning.
The model was trained on grayscale images, potentially discarding color information that is often critical for dermatological diagnosis (e.g., the ABCD rule relies on color asymmetry).
The paper acknowledges limitations regarding the pre-processing step's ability to handle extremely fine hair or complex backgrounds, which could affect real-world deployment robustness.

The comparison with prior works (Table 2) uses accuracy as the sole metric, which is insufficient for evaluating performance on imbalanced medical datasets where sensitivity and specificity are more clinically meaningful.
